title: Farmhouse Bed (Twin Size)
summary: A chunky farmhouse bed with solid 4x4 posts and plank headboard and footboard panels capped in 2x6 — heavy, timeless, and beginner-friendly joinery.
description:
This is the classic farmhouse bed: fat 4x4 posts, headboard and footboard panels made of edge-joined 1x6 planks framed in 1x4, and a broad 2x6 cap on top that gives the whole thing its solid, furniture-store weight. Despite the mass, the joinery is all screws, glue, and brad nails — no mortises — so it's an achievable first bed.

The key to a panel bed looking right is that the plank panels are dead square. After you edge-join the 1x6s and frame them, measure the diagonals corner to corner and adjust until the two measurements match; a panel that's even slightly a parallelogram will fight the posts and leave gaps. Edge-joining is much cleaner with pocket holes if you have a jig, but glue and clamps work too.

Several rail lengths are marked 'measure and cut to fit' on purpose — real lumber varies, and a bed that must accept a mattress can't rely on nominal numbers. Build the panels, dry-assemble to the posts, and take the rail measurements off the actual assembly. The side rails hang on cleats screwed to the posts, reinforced with an L bracket and a straight mending plate at the top corners, and the slats simply lay in and screw down. Leave a gap in the headboard/footboard cleats to accept the side rails, as the steps note.
timeLabel: 8–14 hrs across a weekend, plus finishing

tools.note: A miter saw is preferred for clean square crosscuts on the 4x4s.
Much cleaner for edge-joining the 1x6 panels than face screws.

materials.name: 2x4 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Panel rails, side rails, rail cleats.

cutList.note: Measure and cut to fit the assembled panel.
Measure and cut to fit; space evenly.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Cut the parts the plan fixes now, and leave everything marked measure-and-cut-to-fit until the panels and posts are dry-assembled. From the 1x6: seven headboard planks 30" and seven footboard planks 15" — each 8 ft board gives two 30" and two 15" planks (90" of 96"), and the last 30" and 15" come off the 4 ft board. From the 4x4: two headboard posts 54" and two footboard posts 21", one of each per 8 ft post. From the 2x6: four caps 47-1/2", two per board — that is 95-1/8" of a 96" board once you allow for the kerf, so start from a squared end and cut carefully. From the 2x4: two side rails 80" and two rail cleats 8-1/2", one rail plus one cleat per board, and four top rails 45-1/2", two per board. From the 2x2: two headboard cleats 30" and four footboard cleats 5-1/2". From the 1x10: one 75" side-rail face per board. From the 1x3: twelve slats 38-1/2", two per board. Label everything as it comes off the saw — the headboard and footboard parts differ only in length in several cases, and a 30" plank in the footboard stack is not obvious until the panel is glued up.

steps.title: Build the headboard panel
steps.body: Lay the seven 30" planks edge to edge, best faces up, and shuffle them until the grain and colour read well across the panel — seven 5-1/2" planks make a panel 38-1/2" wide, and that number is what every other headboard part is built around. Edge-join them: pocket holes and 2" wood screws from the back are the clean way if you have the jig, otherwise glue every mating edge and clamp across the panel until dry. Measure the assembled width once it is joined and confirm it really is 38-1/2". Boards run a hair narrow or wide and seven of them compound the error, and this measured width — not the number on paper — is what you cut the 1x4 frame and the top rail to.

steps.title: Frame the panel square
steps.body: Cut the two 1x4 frame pieces to the panel's measured width — 38-1/2" if it came out on the numbers — and attach them across the top and bottom of the panel with 1-1/4" brads and glue, outside edges flush with the panel's sides. Then check the panel is square by measuring both diagonals corner to corner and pushing on the long diagonal until the two match. Do this before the glue sets: a panel that is even slightly a parallelogram will fight the posts and leave a wedge-shaped gap down one side that nothing later in the build can close.

steps.title: Mount the panel to the posts
steps.body: Screw the 2x2 cleats to the inside back edge of the 4x4 posts with 2-1/2" screws, then attach the panel to the cleats with 2-1/2" screws, brads, and glue. (Pocket-hole builders can join the panel straight to the posts and skip the cleats.)

steps.title: Cap the headboard
steps.body: Measure the actual span across the tops of the two posts and cut the 45-1/2" top rail to it — the number should come out at 38-1/2" of panel plus a 3-1/2" post at each side, which is exactly 45-1/2", and if it does not, the panel or the posts have moved and it is worth finding out why now. Attach the rail with 2-1/2" screws and glue. Then set the 47-1/2" 2x6 cap on top of it: 2" longer than the rail, so it overhangs 1" at each end, with the front and back overhangs even. Screw it down from above. This cap is what gives the bed its furniture-store weight, so take the time to get the reveal the same all the way round.

steps.title: Build the footboard the same way
steps.body: Repeat the panel, post, and cap sequence for the shorter footboard. If you're using cleats to hang the side rails, leave a gap in the cleats as shown to accept the rails in a later step.

steps.title: Hang the side rails and slats
steps.body: Screw the two 8-1/2" 2x4 rail cleats to the inside faces of the posts with 2-1/2" screws, both at the same height off the floor — measure from the floor on all four posts rather than from the panel, or the mattress deck will sit twisted. Build each side rail from an 80" 2x4 with a 75" 1x10 face glued and bradded to its outside, set the rails on the cleats, and lock each top corner with a 1-1/2" L bracket and a 3" straight mending plate. Then lay the twelve 38-1/2" slats across and screw them down with 2" screws: twelve 2-1/2" slats use 30" of the 75" the rail faces cover, so spacing them evenly leaves gaps of roughly 4". Work from both ends toward the middle so any accumulated error lands in one gap in the centre rather than at the foot.

images.alt: Twin farmhouse bed with solid posts and plank headboard and footboard
